evidence: we continue to invest in technology. We seek to automate functions previously performed manually, facilitate the ability of customers to engage in financial transactions
We use automation, machine learning, AI and robotic process automation tools to help reduce some risks of human error.
we increasingly use models related to how we do business with customers and for internal process automation that leverage AI/machine learning algorithms

Examples of model use include determining the pricing of various products, identifying potentially fraudulent or suspicious transactions, marketing to potential customers, grading loans and extending credit
the complex way in which the many variables in AI/machine learning models interact, the results of these models are often less interpretable than traditional statistical models
our use of AI/machine learning algorithms is subject to a variety of existing laws and regulations, including intellectual property, privacy...consumer protection and federal equal opportunity laws
flaws in our models that negatively impact our customers or our ability to comply with applicable laws and regulations could negatively affect our reputation or result in fines and penalties

limitations: ai_investment_intensity: No quantified AI-specific spend, headcount, or named vendor/cloud partnerships disclosed in the excerpts; references to technology investment are generic.

ai_narrative_concreteness: Named use cases (fraud detection, credit grading, pricing, marketing) are described but no deployment metrics or quantified outcomes are provided; promotional/risk-disclosure framing rather than operational results.

ai_risk_disclosure_depth: Risk-section material is present (mdna and business sections); risks are company-specific with described mechanisms (model opacity, regulatory exposure, reputational/financial penalties), but no quantification of risk exposure or named governance body specifically for AI risk oversight beyond general CISO reference.
